currency.name: Sudanese pound (SDG)
currency.note: Currency in freefall since the 2023 war began. Hyperinflation, banking system collapsed, USD and gold dominate transactions in contested zones. The Central Bank relocated to Port Sudan.

crypto.note: The Central Bank of Sudan has banned crypto transactions. Infrastructure destroyed by civil war. Irrelevant in current context.

article:
🧭 **Overview**
Sudan is in the middle of one of the world's worst active wars. On April 15, 2023, a power struggle between two generals — Abdel Fattah al-Burhan (Sudanese Armed Forces, SAF) and Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, known as Hemedti (Rapid Support Forces, RSF) — erupted into full-scale civil war across the country. Khartoum, a city of 7 million, became a battlefield. By late 2023, the RSF controlled most of Darfur (where genocidal massacres against the Masalit people were documented) and much of the center. The SAF regained momentum through 2024–2025 using Iranian drones and retook Khartoum by mid-2025. But the war continues in Darfur, Kordofan, and Gezira. The human cost: 400,000+ estimated dead, 13 million displaced internally, 4 million refugees (the world's largest displacement crisis in 2024). GDP contracted 40–48% since 2023. Sudan was already one of the world's poorest and most unstable nations before this war — a legacy of Omar al-Bashir's 30-year Islamist dictatorship, indicted for genocide in Darfur (2003–2005, 300,000 dead), and the 2021 coup that ended Sudan's democratic transition.

👥 **People & vibe**
Sudan is ethnically extraordinarily diverse — Arab, Nubian, Beja, Fur, Nuba, Dinka, and dozens more groups. Islam (~97% Sunni) is the dominant religion, but cultural practices vary enormously between the Arab-influenced Nile Valley north and the African-majority south and west (Darfur, Kordofan). Before the 2023 war, Khartoum was a genuine cosmopolitan city — universities, cafés, a vibrant Nubian music scene, a large educated middle class. That world has been destroyed. Millions of Khartoumers who fled cannot return to homes that no longer exist. The few remaining residents describe a ghost city slowly reawakening amid ruins, with no electricity, water scarcity, and markets barely reopening.

[[MAP]]

🌦️ **Climate & landscape**
Mostly arid and semi-arid. The Nile (White and Blue Nile meet at Khartoum) is the country's lifeline. The north is Nubian desert — ancient pyramids rise from the sand. The center is Sahel savanna. The south (now a separate country — South Sudan seceded in 2011) was tropical. The Nuba Mountains and Jebel Marra volcano in Darfur offer dramatic highland landscapes. Before the war, Sudan had some of Africa's most underrated historical sites: Meroitic pyramids at Meroe, Nubian temples at Soleb, medieval Christian kingdoms at Old Dongola. All now inaccessible.

🏠 **Housing & settling in**
Virtually no expat civilian housing market exists in the conventional sense. Pre-war Khartoum had a functioning expat community (Amarat, Riyad neighborhoods) with villas at $600–1,500/month. That market is now rubble or occupied. Port Sudan — the de facto capital and safe haven on the Red Sea — has seen rents explode: $2,000–3,000/month for apartments that cost $300 before the war. UN and NGO operations are primarily run from Port Sudan, Gedaref, and across borders (Chad, Egypt, South Sudan). No expat civilian relocation to Sudan is advisable or viable in 2025.

💼 **Work & economy**
Before the war: gold mining (Sudan was Africa's 3rd-largest producer), agriculture (gum arabic — Sudan produces 75–80% of global supply), some oil (most oil went with South Sudan's 2011 secession). Today: the formal economy has effectively collapsed. GDP fell 40–48%. 5.2 million jobs lost. The informal economy — smuggling, gold trading, black market currency — has exploded. Humanitarian operations (WFP, UNHCR, ICRC, MSF) are the de facto economy in accessible zones. The gum arabic trade continues through smuggling into Chad and Egypt.

🛂 **Visa & entry**
All Western governments advise against all travel to Sudan and urge nationals to leave. Port Sudan Airport maintains limited international connectivity (Cairo, Istanbul, Doha, Addis Ababa). Entry for humanitarian workers requires coordination with SAF authorities in Port Sudan. No tourism visas being processed in practice.

🏥 **Healthcare**
The healthcare system has effectively collapsed. In Khartoum, 70%+ of health facilities were destroyed, looted, or converted to military uses. 40 million vaccine doses and insulin vials were lost when power cut to cold chain storage. MSF, ICRC, and WHO operate in accessible zones. Cholera, measles, dengue, and malaria are epidemic. Life expectancy (pre-war): ~67 years — now declining sharply. Medical evacuation to Cairo or Nairobi is the only option for serious cases.

🚗 **Transport & mobility**
Khartoum's infrastructure — roads, bridges, power grid, water system — is 70%+ destroyed. Port Sudan is the functional hub: its airport operates, its port handles aid. Road travel between Port Sudan and Khartoum is possible but dangerous (checkpoints, banditry). Travel to Darfur, Kordofan, and Gezira is extremely dangerous or impossible. The SAF controls most air corridors; the RSF controls western ground routes.

🍛 **Food note (national dish)**
The national dish is **Ful medames**: slow-cooked fava beans seasoned with lemon, olive oil, and spices — the definitive Sudanese breakfast eaten at every economic level. **Kisra** (fermented sorghum flatbread, similar to Ethiopian injera) accompanies most meals. **Asida** (thick sorghum porridge) with meat or groundnut sauce is the rural staple. **Shaiyah** (grilled meat) at Nile-side restaurants in Khartoum was the social institution of the pre-war middle class. Sudanese tea culture (spiced with cinnamon and ginger) is deeply embedded.

🔎 **Bottom line**
Sudan is currently one of the most dangerous places on Earth. There is no expat civilian life here — only humanitarian missions operating under extreme security protocols. The SAF's retaking of Khartoum in mid-2025 has opened a cautious phase of reconstruction, but Darfur remains a genocide zone, millions are in famine conditions, and the RSF has not been defeated. Sudan's potential is real — the Nile, historic Nubian civilization, the gum arabic monopoly, gold reserves, agricultural land — but it is buried under decades of dictatorship, civil war, and now the systematic destruction of Africa's third-most-populous country. If you are working here: only through a major international organization, with full security protocols, evacuation plans, and mental health support. This is one of the world's most challenging humanitarian postings.

Expat Score — 1.0 / 10

national_dish.name: Ful medames / Kisra
national_dish.note: Fava beans with lemon and oil — the national breakfast at every income level. Sorghum flatbread (Kisra) with meat or groundnut sauce. Sudanese spiced tea culture is central to social life.

lgbt_acceptance.note: Homosexuality is illegal. Repeat offenders face death penalty under the Penal Code; first offense: flogging + imprisonment. Deeply conservative society. Absolute and total danger. No exceptions.

top_sectors: Gold mining (3rd-largest in Africa pre-war — largely informal now)
Gum arabic (75–80% of global supply — continues via smuggling)

personality.name: Omar al-Bashir
personality.story: Ruled Sudan for 30 years (1989–2019) after an Islamist coup. Indicted by the International Criminal Court for genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes in Darfur — the first sitting head of state to be indicted by the ICC. He killed an estimated 300,000 people in Darfur (2003–2005) using the Janjaweed militias — the predecessors of today's RSF, commanded by his own ally Hemedti. He was overthrown in 2019 by the same generals he had empowered. He died in prison in January 2025 awaiting an ICC extradition that never came.

cards.title: 🔺 Nubian pyramids: forgotten civilization
cards.text: Sudan has more ancient pyramids than Egypt — over 200, scattered across the Nubian desert at Meroe, Nuri, and El-Kurru. The Meroitic Kingdom (the Kingdom of Kush) ruled northeastern Africa for 1,000 years, conquered Egypt in 712 BC, and built a sophisticated civilization with its own script (Meroitic — still only partially decoded). These pyramids are UNESCO World Heritage sites. They were visited by fewer than 2,000 tourists per year even before the war — among the least-visited major archaeological sites in the world.

cards.title: ⚔️ Two generals, one catastrophe
cards.text: The 2023 war's proximate cause was a power struggle between two men who had been allies in every coup and atrocity since 2003. Al-Burhan (SAF head) and Hemedti (RSF commander) overthrew Sudan's democratic transition together in 2021. They fell out over the RSF's integration into the regular army — Hemedti refused to dissolve his 100,000-strong parallel force. On April 15, 2023, they went to war. Both are accused of war crimes. Both claim to represent Sudan's future. The country they destroyed between them had 48 million people.

cards.title: 🌿 Gum arabic: invisible monopoly
cards.text: Sudan produces 75–80% of the world's gum arabic — the acacia tree resin used in everything from Coca-Cola (stabilizer E414) to pharmaceutical capsule coatings to postage stamp adhesive. Almost every packaged food product globally contains Sudanese gum arabic. During the war, the trade continues via smuggling through Chad and Egypt because global supply chains depend on it and no alternative source exists. It is one of the world's most consequential commodities that virtually nobody has heard of.

cards.title: 🌊 Nile civilization: the original source
cards.text: The Nile — both White (from Uganda/Lake Victoria) and Blue (from Ethiopia/Lake Tana) — meets at Khartoum. Sudan's northern Nile Valley was home to the Kerma culture (2500 BC), Napatan Kingdom, and Meroitic civilization — Nubian cultures that predated and influenced Pharaonic Egypt. The Great Dam of Merowe (2009) and the now-contested GERD dam in Ethiopia have reshaped the Nile's flow politics. Egypt, Sudan, and Ethiopia are locked in a water rights dispute that could outlast the civil war.

cards.title: ☁️ Darfur: genocide that keeps recurring
cards.text: Darfur — the size of France in western Sudan — has experienced two genocides in 20 years. In 2003–2005, Bashir's Janjaweed militias killed 300,000 and displaced 2 million in what the US government officially recognized as genocide. The same militias, rebranded as the RSF under Hemedti, returned in 2023 and conducted systematic massacres of the Masalit people in El Geneina and throughout West Darfur. The UN High Commissioner called it a repeat of the 2003 genocide. The world watched again.

cards.title: ✊ The Revolution of Smiles
cards.text: In December 2018, Sudanese citizens began protests over bread prices that grew into a revolution. Women — many in white tobes — were at the forefront. By April 2019, Bashir was overthrown. The images of the Khartoum sit-in (massive crowds, singing, candlelight) became icons of peaceful resistance. A transitional civilian-military council governed briefly. Then in October 2021, the same generals who had 'supported' the revolution crushed it in a coup, jailing civilian prime minister Hamdok. Sudan's democratic spring lasted two years — then two generals destroyed it, and then each other.
